idioms.definition: To do or say something to relieve tension or awkwardness in a social situation and make people feel more comfortable
examples: He told a joke to break the ice at the start of the meeting.
Playing a team game is a great way to break the ice with new colleagues.
The host broke the ice by asking everyone to introduce themselves.
idioms.usageTip: Often used in professional or social situations where people don't know each other well. An 'icebreaker' is a noun form of this concept.

idioms.definition: To start an activity or process, especially one that will continue for a while
examples: Let's get the ball rolling by looking at this week's agenda.
She got the ball rolling on the project by scheduling the first meeting.
Someone needs to get the ball rolling, or nothing will ever happen.
idioms.usageTip: Often used in business or project contexts. Similar to 'kick things off' or 'start the ball rolling'.

idioms.definition: To agree with someone; to have the same opinion or view
examples: My father and I don't always see eye to eye on politics.
The two managers finally saw eye to eye after a long discussion.
It's rare to see eye to eye with everyone in a team.
idioms.usageTip: Usually used in the negative: 'don't/didn't see eye to eye'. When used positively, it often implies that agreement was finally reached.

idioms.definition: To remove the tension or misunderstanding from a situation by discussing things openly
examples: After the argument, they sat down to clear the air.
We need to have a meeting and clear the air about what happened.
Clearing the air helped them work together more effectively.
idioms.usageTip: Use this when there is tension or a misunderstanding that needs to be resolved through honest conversation.

idioms.definition: Thinking in the same way; having the same understanding or attitude about something
examples: We work really well together – we're on the same wavelength.
The two designers were immediately on the same wavelength.
It helps to be on the same wavelength as your manager.
idioms.usageTip: Suggests a natural, effortless connection or mutual understanding. Often used in creative or collaborative contexts.

idioms.idiom: get on like a house on fire
idioms.definition: To like each other very much and become friends very quickly; to have an extremely good relationship
examples: I was nervous about meeting her family, but we got on like a house on fire.
The two new team members got on like a house on fire from day one.
My flatmate and I get on like a house on fire – we never argue.
idioms.usageTip: Always positive – it means an instant and enthusiastic friendship. The 'fire' suggests the speed and intensity of the connection.

fillInExercises.sentence: The team leader told a funny story at the start to ___ at the conference.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Break the ice' means to ease tension or awkwardness at the beginning of a social or professional gathering.

fillInExercises.sentence: We've had a difficult week, but I think it's time to sit down and ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Clear the air' means to resolve tension or misunderstanding through open discussion.

fillInExercises.sentence: I introduced myself and suggested we start planning – just to ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get the ball rolling' means to start an activity or process.

fillInExercises.sentence: My sister and I don't always ___ about how to raise our kids, but we respect each other.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'See eye to eye' means to agree with someone on a topic.

fillInExercises.sentence: My new neighbour and I ___. We ended up talking for three hours on the first day we met.
options: got on like a house on fire
fillInExercises.correct: got on like a house on fire
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get on like a house on fire' means to have an immediate, very positive connection with someone.

fillInExercises.sentence: It's really easy working with her – we're completely ___.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'On the same wavelength' means thinking alike and sharing the same understanding.

lines.speaker: Helen
lines.text: Right, we have three new people joining the team today. Should we do something to [break the ice|break the ice]?

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Good idea. Maybe a quick round of introductions and a team lunch to [get the ball rolling|get the ball rolling].

lines.speaker: Helen
lines.text: Perfect. I want them to feel comfortable straight away.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: I think once they start working together, they'll be [on the same wavelength|on the same wavelength] in no time.

lines.speaker: Helen
lines.text: I hope so. Last time we hired someone new, they didn't [see eye to eye|see eye to eye] with Marcus at all.

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: Yes, that was awkward. But they eventually managed to [clear the air|clear the air] after a good conversation.

lines.speaker: Helen
lines.text: True. And by the end of the project, they [got on like a house on fire|got on like a house on fire].

lines.speaker: James
lines.text: People just need a bit of time. I'm sure it'll be fine with this new group.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: I'm glad you agreed to meet. I feel like we need to [clear the air|clear the air] after last week.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: I agree. I don't want things to stay awkward between us.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: We've always been [on the same wavelength|on the same wavelength] before, so I was surprised we clashed so badly.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Honestly, we just don't [see eye to eye|see eye to eye] on how to present data to clients. And that's okay.

lines.speaker: Sophie
lines.text: You're right. We don't always have to agree. Let's just [get the ball rolling|get the ball rolling] on the new proposal and move forward.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Deal. And I'm sure the clients will [get on like a house on fire|get on like a house on fire] with the new team anyway.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.idiom: get on like a house on fire
pairs.meaning: become friends very quickly and enthusiastically

quizExercises.sentence: Let me ___ by sharing a few thoughts on what we want to achieve today.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Get the ball rolling' means to start or initiate a process.

quizExercises.sentence: My co-writer and I are ___. We barely need to explain ideas to each other.
quizExercises.explanation: 'On the same wavelength' means naturally thinking alike without much need for explanation.

quizExercises.question: In which situation would you 'clear the air'?
options: After a misunderstanding that has created tension between two people
When you want to start a new project
When two people immediately get along very well
quizExercises.explanation: 'Clear the air' is used when you need to talk openly to resolve tension or a misunderstanding.

quizExercises.question: Which activity is a classic example of 'breaking the ice'?
options: Sending a long email to explain a difficult situation
Playing a fun introduction game with people who don't know each other
Finishing a project ahead of schedule
quizExercises.explanation: 'Breaking the ice' involves an activity or comment that eases the awkwardness when people first meet.
